title: 🎙️ Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling' — how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Most genuine arts/culture/media journalism should land well above 0, even without a Cariboo tie-in:
- 80-100: Cariboo/Williams Lake-specific arts and culture news — community arts council grants or murals, local festivals, Indigenous film/storytelling, community theatre, museum/heritage programs, a Cariboo author or artist receiving an award, community radio or local publishing.
- 55-79: Rural BC or Canadian arts/culture journalism without a specific Cariboo tie — CBC Arts features on Indigenous storytelling, regional festivals, community media projects, heritage preservation, creative-economy stories in small towns, digital storytelling tools and platforms.
- 30-54: General arts, culture, media, or entertainment journalism with real creative/storytelling substance but no rural or community angle (national awards shows, mainstream film/TV/music industry coverage, publishing industry news).
- 15-29: Tangentially creative content (a tech feature that touches on media/publishing/streaming) or local Cariboo civic news with no arts/culture angle.
- 0-14: No arts, culture, media, or storytelling dimension at all.

Strong fits include: community arts journalism (arts councils, local festivals, heritage programs), oral history and cultural preservation projects, Indigenous film and storytelling, community theatre and local media, rural arts funding and the creative economy in small towns, and digital storytelling platforms and tools. Also strong fits: community media tech (podcasting, streaming, local publishing), creative tech economy, open-source arts tools, Cariboo arts festivals using digital outreach, animation/film/media production in rural BC, local radio/podcast infrastructure, and how rural communities use technology for creative expression. A story about a Cariboo community mural, a CBC Arts feature on BC Indigenous storytelling, a rural arts council receiving grants, or a Cariboo author/artist shortlisted for an award is a strong fit even without an explicit technology angle — cultural journalism matters to this audience. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): medical and health research with no cultural/storytelling angle; working lands and resource economy stories with no cultural framing; wildlife and nature stories without creative or community media context; data centre infrastructure and AI investment news; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime coverage; financial market and stock movement stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Williams Lake Tribune] Central Chilcotin Rehabilitation Ltd. wins Indigenous Small Business Award
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

## Central Chilcotin Rehabilitation Ltd. wins Indigenous Small Business Award Published 10:50 am Thursday, July 16, 2026 **By Williams Lake Tribune Staff** The large-scale forest rehabilitation Palmer Project by Central Chilcotin Rehabilitation Ltd. (Liam Hall/Kiwiman Creative photo) Central Chilcotin Rehabilitation Ltd. (CCR) has received the Indigenous Small Business Award from the Canadian Council for Indigenous Business (CCIB). According to a July 14 press release from CCIB, the award recognizes the company’s leadership in Indigenous-led forestry, forest rehabilitation, wildfire risk

items.title: [Williams Lake Tribune] Norma Carol Mitchell
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

# Norma Carol Mitchell photo1 1/2 photo1 photo2 Published July 16, 2026 **January 7, 1944 – July 6, 2026** Norma Carol Mitchell, 82, of Williams Lake, passed away peacefully in her sleep on July 6th, 2026. Born in North Vancouver on January 7th, 1944, to Norm and Isabel Woodard, Norma grew up attending North Vancouver High. Her passion for helping others led her to pursue a career in healthcare, completing her registered nursing education at Vancouver General Hospital and later earning her Bachelor of Science in Nursing from the University of Victoria. Norma’s life was defined by dedication

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] National Gallery of Armenia in Yerevan, Armenia
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

The National Gallery of Armenia is the largest fine arts museum in Armenia and home to the most comprehensive collection of Armenian art in the world.

Founded in 1921, the Gallery preserves more than 42,000 works of Armenian and international fine art and decorative-applied arts. Its collections include masterpieces by world-renowned artists such as Hovhannes Aivazovsky, Vardges Sureniants, Arshile Gorky, Marc Chagall, Wassily Kandinsky, Donatello, Tintoretto, and many others. The Gallery also preservs unique fragments of medieval Armenian church frescoes from Armenia and Armenian cultural centers abroad, including Aruch, Lmbatavank, Tatev, Haghpat, Ani, Talin, Akhtala, Kobayr, Meghri, and Crimea. In addition, it houses an exceptional archive of approximately 200,000 items, including photographs, notebooks, letters, articles, personal belongings, and documentary materials related to prominent Armenian and international artists and significant cultural events.

Today, the National Gallery of Armenia is the only museum in the region distinguished by the breadth and scale of its collection of world fine art. It serves as a key center for national cultural memory, creative thought, and artistic values, contributing to the international recognition of Armenian art and fostering intercultural dialogue while strengthening Armenia’s place on the global cultural map.

The National Gallery of Armenia is housed on eight floors of a building recognized as a monument of national significance and operates 14 branches in Yerevan and across the regions of Armenia.

Drawing upon international best practices and adapting its activities to the standards of the contemporary museum, the Gallery continues the mission initiated more than a century ago: preserving, studying, and transmitting Armenia’s spiritual and cultural heritage to future generations.

The collections of the National Gallery of Armenia represent an immense intellectual, emotional, and cultural resource, offering both Armenian and international visitors the opportunity to discover and engage with the treasures of Armenian and world art.
items.summary: The National Gallery of Armenia is the largest fine arts museum in Armenia and home to the most comprehensive collection of Armenian art in the world. Founded in 1921, the Gallery preserves more than 42,000 works of Armenian and international fine art and decorative-applied arts. Its collections
items._excerpt: The National Gallery of Armenia is the largest fine arts museum in Armenia and home to the most comprehensive collection of Armenian art in the world. items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Porcelain Room in Aranjuez, Spain
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Visitors to Aranjuez often come for its royal gardens and the grandeur of the Royal Palace of Aranjuez. Yet one of the palace's most astonishing treasures is tucked away in a corner room that feels more like a theatrical illusion than a royal chamber.

Completed in 1765, the Porcelain Room was the first major masterpiece created by the Royal Porcelain Factory established under Charles III of Spain. Designed by Giuseppe Gricci, the room transformed an earlier royal cabinet into a dazzling showcase of Rococo craftsmanship. Aside from a marble floor dating to the reign of Isabella Farnese, nearly every visible surface is covered with porcelain.

The walls and ceiling are sheathed in white porcelain plaques animated by brightly colored reliefs. Chinese figures dressed in elaborate robes mingle with monkeys, exotic birds, dragons, reptiles, flowers, and musical instruments. Mirrors framed by porcelain candelabra multiply the effect, creating the sensation of stepping inside a three-dimensional porcelain painting.

The room reflects Europe's eighteenth-century fascination with imagined visions of Asia. Inspired by French Rococo taste, Italian craftsmanship, and German porcelain traditions, its decoration combines luxury, whimsy, and technical virtuosity in equal measure.

The most remarkable feature hangs overhead: a porcelain chandelier shaped like a palm tree. A Chinese figure clings to its trunk while a monkey imitates him nearby. More than a decorative room, the Porcelain Room is a royal fever dream preserved in ceramic—a hidden masterpiece that remains one of the most extraordinary interiors in Spain.
items.summary: Visitors to Aranjuez often come for its royal gardens and the grandeur of the Royal Palace of Aranjuez. Yet one of the palace's most astonishing treasures is tucked away in a corner room that feels more like a theatrical illusion than a royal chamber. Completed in 1765, the Porcelain Room was the

items.title: [Kottke.org] Amazing Newspaper Ad Highlighting Period Poverty
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

To draw attention to the millions of girls who cannot afford period products, the MENstruation Foundation ran a unique advertisement in three major South African newspapers.

Readers of The Star, The Mercury and Cape Times opened their newspapers this week to find something deeply uncomfortable: period blood stains on the front page, seeping through to the pages beneath — as if the newspaper itself had been used as a sanitary pad.

South African schoolgirls suffering from period poverty are forced to use newspapers, rags, and even cow dung in place of sanitary pads. Here’s a short video of what the ad looks like as you page through the paper, culminating in a full page highlighting the foundation’s free sanitary pad dispensers placed in schools.

Maybe one of the best advertisements I’ve ever seen? (thx, caroline)

Tags: advertising · health · video

Readers of The Star, The Mercury and Cape Times opened their newspapers this week to find something deeply uncomfortable:

items.title: [Boing Boing] Scientists baked sourdough with yeast from a 5,300-year-old mummy
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Ötzi the Iceman died in the Alps roughly 5,300 years ago, and researchers have now used yeast recovered from his frozen body to bake sourdough bread. According to Smithsonian magazine, a team at Eurac Research's Institute for Mummy Studies found four cold-adapted yeast strains clinging to the mummified remains, one of them a Glaciozyma that came to dominate the culture. — Read the rest

The post Scientists baked sourdough with yeast from a 5,300-year-old mummy appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [CBC Arts] Mark Hamill's Star Wars lightsaber sells for record-breaking price at auction
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Luke Skywalker's lightsaber, one of the most iconic pieces of film memorabilia, sold at auction for $3.75 million US (roughly $5.15 million Cdn), Heritage Auctions shared on Wednesday. The prop was constructed by Star Wars' makeup artist Stuart Freeborn, and includes the model-cast of actor Mark Hamill's hand that detached alongside it in the iconic scene.

items.title: [Boing Boing] The NY Times' crossword editor kept building puzzles from his hospital bed
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Four years ago, New York Times crossword editor Will Shortz had a stroke. "I was making puzzles in bed while I was in the hospital," he told A.J. Jacobs in a recent interview. 

Recovery has been slow. He still does daily physical therapy and figures he may never be all the way back. — Read the rest

The post The NY Times' crossword editor kept building puzzles from his hospital bed appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [The Verge] Why are people buying so many CDs?
items.content_html:

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Celestia in Laporte Township, Pennsylvania
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Today, visitors to Celestia will find little more than a forest, a few traces of old foundations, and a historical marker along Route 42. In the mid-1800s, however, this mountaintop was intended to become something far more ambitious: a sacred city built in preparation for Christ's return.

Founded by religious leader Peter E. Armstrong, Celestia was established as a community of millennialists who believed they were creating a wilderness refuge for the coming Kingdom of God. Armstrong envisioned a mountaintop "City of Heaven" where his followers would live apart from the outside world and await the fulfillment of biblical prophecy.

The community became famous for an unusual legal decision. In 1864, Armstrong deeded the land to Almighty God and "His heirs in Jesus Messiah." Unfortunately, the new owner never paid the property taxes. Sullivan County eventually seized and sold the land for unpaid taxes, and the dream of Celestia slowly unraveled.

By the time Armstrong died in 1887, the settlement had largely been abandoned. Houses collapsed, fields returned to forest, and the mountain swallowed the community almost completely. What remains today is less a ghost town than a ghost idea: the fading footprint of a utopian religious experiment hidden in the Pennsylvania Wilds.
items.summary: Today, visitors to Celestia will find little more than a forest, a few traces of old foundations, and a historical marker along Route 42. items.title: [MacRumors] Apple Closes Unlocked iPhone Loophole for T-Mobile and Verizon Financing
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Carrier-financed iPhones purchased from Apple will soon be locked to the carrier, ending a workaround customers used to purchase an unlocked iPhone on a payment plan.


Until the rule change, buying an iPhone from Apple and opting for financing through Verizon or T-Mobile meant you would get an iPhone not locked to either carrier's network. That's no longer the case, and now iPhones financed through Verizon or T-Mobile will not be able to be used with a different network. AT&T-financed smartphones were always locked to the AT&T network.

Apple's new policy was highlighted on Reddit after a user saw a new "Will my iPhone be unlocked?" FAQ item.
In most cases, yes. An iPhone purchased from Apple is unlocked. Once your new iPhone is activated, it remains unlocked, which means you can use it with any carrier that provides service for iPhone. However, if you choose to finance an iPhone through the AT&T Installment Plan, T-Mobile Equipment Installment Plan, or Verizon Device Payment Program, your iPhone will be locked to the carrier until paid in full.

An unlocked smartphone means it can be used with any carrier, while a locked smartphone is limited to the carrier it was purchased through. Locked smartphones can be problematic when traveling internationally, because activating a second eSIM for another network is blocked.

Apple hasn't said why it's making the change, but buyers were able to use carrier trade-ins and promotional discounts to get an unlocked iPhone. Some buyers may have purchased iPhones and sold them off without making the carrier payments, and carrier locking is a standard practice to prevent unpaid devices from being resold.

Apple's checkout process still says iPhones financed through T-Mobile and Verizon are unlocked, so the new policy may not have gone into effect yet.

iPhones purchased outright or with Apple Card Monthly Installments remain unlocked from the time of purchase. When iPhones purchased through carrier plans are paid off, they are unlocked.
Tags: T-Mobile, Verizon

This article, "Apple Closes Unlocked iPhone Loophole for T-Mobile and Verizon Financing" first appeared on MacRumors.com

Discuss this article in our forums

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] ‘I’m sorry but I’m unable to speak’: hero of India’s Cockroach party weakens on 19th day of hunger strike
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

The climate activist and engineer Sonam Wangchuk, who has become the figurehead of anti-government protests in Delhi, is resisting calls to end his fast until the education minister resigns

As night falls on day 18 of his hunger strike, the lack of any sustenance except water shows. It is 7pm and Sonam Wangchuk looks weak. A physiotherapist sits beside him on the stage massaging his arms to soothe his aching joints. Propped up against pillows and bolsters, Wangchuk whispers: “I’m sorry, but I’m unable to speak.”

The activist and engineer has lost close to 9kg from a body that was spare and lean to begin with. Doctors say it is around this stage of a hunger strike that the body enters a state of severe starvation, breaking down fat and muscle, resulting in extreme weakness, impaired brain function and electrolyte imbalance.

Continue reading...

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Uganda calls for travel restrictions to be lifted after last Ebola patient discharged
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Country begins 42-day countdown to outbreak being declared officially over, as numbers continue to rise in neighbouring Democratic Republic of the Congo

Uganda has started lobbying countries to lift Ebola-related travel restrictions after discharging its last confirmed Ebola patient from hospital.

The discharge of a Congolese national from the Mulago national referral hospital’s isolation centre in Kampala on Thursday triggered the start of a 42-day countdown required by the World Health Organization before Uganda can officially be declared Ebola-free, provided no new infections are detected.

Continue reading...
items.summary: Country begins 42-day countdown to outbreak being declared officially over, as numbers continue to rise in neighbouring Democratic Republic of the Congo Uganda has started lobbying countries to lift Ebola-related travel restrictions after discharging its last confirmed Ebola patient from hospital.
items._excerpt: Country begins 42-day countdown to outbreak being declared officially over, as numbers continue to rise in neighbouring Democratic Republic of the Congo Uganda has started lobbying countries to lift Ebola-related travel restrictions after discharging its last confirmed Ebola patient from hospital. The discharge of a Congolese national from the Mulago national referral hospital’s isolation centre in Kampala on Thursday triggered the start of a 42-day countdown required by the World Health Organization before Uganda can officially be declared Ebola-free, provided no new infections are detected.
